title: Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment
description: Prerequisite: CI 6003 .  Examination of different pedagogical approaches to the teaching and learning process in schools, with emphasis on the development of curriculum for classroom instruction, evaluation, organization, and management. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Engineering Education Theory and Practice
description: This course is designed to provide graduate students with an opportunity to acquire foundational knowledge of theories of teaching and learning in engineering education. The course contains principles of learner-centered and evidence-based pedagogy and assessment in pre-engineering and engineering learning environments. This course serves as an opportunity to gain knowledge about the role of engineering educators as teachers, mentors, and advisors through a focused examination of theory, disciplinary literacies, and empirical research in engineering education. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Mentored Teaching in Engineering
description: This course allows students the opportunity for a deeper understanding of teaching and learning through practice, feedback, and reflection as performed regularly in assigned teaching duties. Educational goals and objectives are identified, and engaging and meaningful activities are designed to address the learning goals using asset-based, student-centered curricular strategies. Students have the opportunity to develop assessments of the classroom activities and methods they use to support student learning. Reflection is used as a central method for learning and formatively evaluating the educator’s practice. Topics include equity in education, inclusive teaching, grading and assessment, engagement activities, and cultural relevance. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Professional Development in Engineering Education
description:
This course provides students with an opportunity to acquire an awareness of the
Standards for Professional Development for Teachers of Engineering. Students will have an opportunity to practice strategies for designing and delivering professional development experiences for teachers and instructors of engineering. Students will be made aware of future careers and professional advancement opportunities at institutions of higher education, K-12 settings, corporate training, and formal and informal educational environments. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Building Spatial Reasoning Foundations for Future Engineers
description: This course provides students the opportunity to explore research and pedagogical approaches to integrating spatial visualization as a skill to support students and professionals within the science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) curricula. Instruction and exercises will be provided to allow students the opportunity to practice some spatial visualization skills. The course will also allow for the exploration of some technologies that can support various forms of visual representation of data. Examples include medical imaging, communication technology, transportation technology, and energy and power technology. (Formerly titled: "Teaching Engineering through Spatial Visualization".) This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Engineering for Positive Social Impact
description: This course investigates the societal issues that emerge as technologies become more complex and interconnected. Drawing on Humanistic Engineering and Systems Engineering, students examine how design choices influence communities, systems, and long-term outcomes. Students will explore professional case studies of humanistic engineering and collaborate with undergraduate teams working on community-focused engineering projects. Through these experiences, they learn to connect technical work with human and societal considerations and to integrate these perspectives into their own teaching, professional portfolios, and future courses. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Preparing Ethical Problem-Solvers in Engineering
description: This course provides students with an opportunity to acquire foundational perspectives on engineering ethics and social responsibility in relationship to individuals, industry, and the public welfare in both education and practice. The course places emphasis on the unified nature of ethics, philosophy, morality, legal responsibility, and social issues. (Formerly titled: "Engineering Social Responsibility and Ethics".) This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Human Centered Design and the Impact of Modern Technologies
description: This course explores the issues faced by society as technology becomes an integral part of human life. The course provides students with opportunities to think critically, practically, creatively, and responsively about technological and social challenges; and encourages them to examine potential solutions to challenges of their own. The course also explores and discusses the socio-technological and user interplay in engineering design. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Foundations of Engineering Education Research Methodologies
description: This course provides students with an introduction to the field of engineering education research. The course offers students the opportunity to critically explore, analyze, and apply theoretical and conceptual frameworks to guide and conduct engineering education research. The course also allows students opportunities to examine the processes to engage in ethical research practices in engineering spaces. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Advanced Engineering Education Research Methodologies
description: Prerequisite: EGR 6653 or instructor approval.  This course offers an in-depth examination of research study designs and methodologies in engineering education research. An exploration of current and emerging research methods in engineering education research are analyzed, critiqued, and evaluated throughout the course. The course also provides students with theoretical literature on quantitative, qualitative, and mixed-methods research approaches from different fields such as psychology, sociology, anthropology, and STEM education to explore their relation to engineering education. Course may be repeated for credit when topics vary. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Advanced Topics in Interdisciplinary STEM Education
description: Topics and critical issues in interdisciplinary STEM education. Topics include a focus on (1) research and development of innovative STEM learning and emerging STEM learning environments both in and out of school settings and (2) research that advances the field of formal and informal STEM Education. Course may be repeated for credit when topics vary. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Engineering Education Practicum
description: The purpose of this course is to expose students to challenge-based instructional pedagogies under the supervision of the faculty advisor. This course is a practical introduction to engineering education that considers technical and social justice challenges in the community. The course allows for the development of transforming leadership competencies, connects students to their surrounding community through an engineering lens, and provides an experiential and collaborative learning experience that integrates knowledge. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Graduate Project
description: Students will have the opportunity to demonstrate acquired skills in engineering education by carrying out a major culminating engineering education project using the engineering design process. This semester-long project must have the prior approval of a supervising faculty member. Credit will be awarded upon successful submission of a written report. May be repeated once for elective credit, but not more than 6 hours will apply to the master’s degree. Enrollment is required each term in which the project is in progress. This course has Differential Tuition. Course Fee: DL01 $75.

title: Special Problems in Engineering Education
description: A course offering the opportunity for specialized study in engineering education for instruction in either a college/university setting, a K‐12 educational classroom, or a workforce skill development space. Topics may include focus on (1) research and development of innovative Engineering Education pedagogies (e.g., Active Learning, Digital Pedagogy, Personalized Learning, etc.) and (2) skill development in research grant proposal development for Engineering and Engineering Education. (Formerly titled: "Special Problems: Becoming an Engineering Educator".) This course may be repeated for credit when topics vary. This course has Differential Tuition.

title: Master's Thesis Research
description: Prerequisite: Master’s student standing and consent of the instructor and the Graduate Advisor of Record. Students pursuing the thesis option will receive guidance through this course and the opportunity to propose, execute, summarize, and defend a research project. This course has Differential Tuition.
